comments.quote: For IID datasets, Per-FedAvg has the best results except on EMNIST dataset.
comments.explanation: Reviewing the IID columns in Table 1 reveals that Per-FedAvg actually leads on EMNIST (91.27%), CIFAR100 (35.03%), and CIFAR10 (74.00%). It is only surpassed by SPFL-w on MNIST (98.90% vs 98.89%) and CIFAR10 (74.04% vs 74.00%). The text incorrectly singles out EMNIST as the exception. Rewrite the sentence to align the prose with the reported numerical values.

comments.title: False claim of universal outperformance over FedAvg on IID data
comments.quote: Compared to FedAvg and independent training of each client in Appendix B, SPFL outperforms both no matter on IID datasets or non-IID datasets.
comments.explanation: The absolute claim that SPFL outperforms FedAvg on all IID datasets is directly contradicted by the EMNIST IID entry in Table 1, where SPFL records 89.53% accuracy against FedAvg's 90.41%. This discrepancy weakens the stated conclusion. Rewrite the conclusion to correctly account for the single case where the baseline performs better.

comments.title: Table accuracy values contradict bolding convention
comments.quote: The best and second to the best performance are bolded and underlined, respectively.
comments.explanation: The table caption claims the best results are bolded, but PLGA's CIFAR100 client accuracy (20.73±1.15) is numerically lower than LGA's (20.74±1.15), and its server accuracy (20.84±1.12) trails FedAvg(Async) at 20.98±0.93. Additionally, these marginal differences lie entirely within the reported standard deviations, rendering the best designation statistically unsupported. The current formatting misleads readers into believing PLGA dominates across all metrics. Rewrite the table caption to accurately reflect the numerical values.

comments.title: Ambiguous straggler missing period assignment
comments.quote: gglers in  $S$ , we let them missing server aggregation round periodically with different fixed missing period as  $1, 2, \ldots, |S|$ , where the values correspond to the number of server iteration round
comments.explanation: The description of the straggler simulation lacks a clear mapping between the set S and the assigned missing periods. It is ambiguous whether each of the five stragglers receives a distinct period from the sequence 1 to 5, or if periods are assigned randomly or uniformly. The subsequent example describes a specific 2-period cycle but does not generalize the schedule for the entire experiment. Specify the exact assignment rule and provide a concrete participation timeline for the |S|=5 configuration used in Table 2 so readers can replicate the asynchronous environment.

comments.title: Overstated performance claims relative to baselines
comments.quote: The experiment results demonstrate that the proposed algorithms have excellent performances on non-IID data, while still maintaining the state of the art performance on IID data.
comments.explanation: The conclusion asserts that the proposed methods achieve state of the art performance, yet the experimental evaluation only compares SPFL and PLGA against older baselines published between 2017 and 2020. The personalized FL literature has since advanced significantly, with established methods like FedFOMO, Ditto, FedRep, and Per-Adam now serving as standard references. Without comparing against these modern approaches, the claim of SOTA performance is unsupported by the reported experiments. Rewrite the claim to accurately reflect the experimental scope.

comments.title: Formal results omit PLGA and asynchronous FL details
comments.quote: We tackled the statistical and system heterogeneity in FL in two scenarios, namely synchronous FL and asynchronous FL with SPFL and PLGA algorithms, respectively.
comments.explanation: The discussion explicitly claims that the paper addresses both synchronous and asynchronous federated learning through the SPFL and PLGA algorithms. The provided formal results section, however, only details Algorithm 1 for SPFL and its initialization steps. There is no formal definition, algorithmic specification, or theoretical treatment of PLGA or the asynchronous update mechanism. Consequently, the formal results do not support the claim that both scenarios are formally addressed in this section. To resolve this, either integrate the formal specification of PLGA and the asynchronous protocol into the results section, or revise the discussion to state that PLGA and asynchronous FL are evaluated empirically without formal theoretical treatment in the main text.

comments.title: Vague reference to ResNet adaptation
comments.quote: The stage strategy of ResNet is further applied to improve the performance of FL.
comments.explanation: This sentence provides no actionable information about the proposed method. Stage strategy of ResNet is undefined and does not clarify whether the authors use a specific network depth, apply stage-wise aggregation, or modify residual connections. Without a precise description of the architectural change or training schedule, the claim adds no technical value. Specify the actual architectural configuration or training protocol being adopted.

comments.title: Notation clash between tilde and hat symbols
comments.quote:
normalized as $\tilde{S}(i,j)$. $\tilde{S}(i,j)$, the softmax version of similarity relationship between $C_{i}$ and $C_{j}$, is defined as:

$\hat{S}(i,j)=\frac{e^{S(i,
comments.explanation: The text introduces the softmax-normalized similarity as $\tilde{S}(i,j)$ but then defines it using the symbol $\hat{S}(i,j)$ in Equation (5). This notation clash creates ambiguity when the symbol is referenced later in Equations (6) and (7), where $\tilde{S}_s$ is used. Readers cannot reliably track which symbol corresponds to the normalized similarity without guessing. Rewrite Equation (5) to use $\tilde{S}(i,j)$ instead of $\hat{S}(i,j)$ for consistency.

comments.title: Undefined cosine similarity for zero-gradient updates
comments.explanation: The cosine similarity formula in Eq. (3) becomes undefined when either client's local update vector is zero, which can happen due to empty local data or convergence. The algorithm description lacks a numerical stabilization step, such as adding a small $\epsilon$ to the denominator or defining a default similarity value for zero-norm gradients. Without this, the softmax normalization in Eq. (5) will fail during training. Add a stabilization term $\epsilon$ to the denominator in Equation (3).

comments.title: Inconsistent weighting scheme breaks normalization
comments.explanation: The aggregation rule in Eq. (4) combines the softmax-normalized similarity $\tilde{S}(i,j)$ with the dataset proportion $\frac{|D_j|}{|D|}$. Since $\tilde{S}(i,j)$ already sums to 1 over $j$ for a fixed $i$, multiplying by $\frac{|D_j|}{|D|}$ breaks the normalization and arbitrarily scales the update magnitude by dataset sizes. This dual-weighting scheme lacks theoretical justification and may cause unstable convergence when data sizes vary significantly across clients. Remove the dataset proportion factor $\frac{|D_j|}{|D|}$ from Equation (4) or provide a theoretical justification for combining it with the softmax-normalized similarity.

coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Coarse (deepseek-v4-flash)
coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback:
The paper tackles an important problem—personalized federated learning under both statistical and system heterogeneity—and proposes two algorithms (SPFL and PLGA) that use gradient similarity to generate client-specific models. The empirical results show consistent improvements over several baselines on four datasets. However, the paper has significant weaknesses in its theoretical framing, algorithmic justification, experimental rigor, and clarity of presentation that limit its contribution.

Overview issues:

- Gradient similarity metric is not robust to local training epochs and batch sampling variance: The gradient similarity $S(i,j)$ in (3) is computed from the difference between the global model and the model after $E$ local epochs. This difference is not a pure gradient—it is the accumulated effect of multiple SGD steps. The paper does not discuss how the number of local epochs $E$, the batch size $B$, or the learning rate $\beta$ affect this quantity. Two clients with identical data distributions could have very different $g_i^t$ if they use different batch orders or if $E>1$. The paper should either justify why this aggregated difference is a reliable similarity measure, or modify the algorithm to compute gradients on a common reference batch. Without this, the similarity matrix may be noisy and unreliable, especially in the non-IID settings where the paper claims its main advantage.

- No theoretical convergence guarantees for personalized models: The paper states in the conclusion that theoretical proof of convergence is future work. For a top-venue paper proposing new optimization algorithms, this is a critical omission. The algorithms involve several non-standard components: a softmax-normalized similarity matrix that changes over time, a stage-wise aggregation, and a first-order Taylor expansion to approximate straggler models. Without any convergence analysis, it is unclear whether the algorithms are guaranteed to converge, under what conditions, and at what rate. The paper should provide at least a proof of convergence to a stationary point under standard assumptions (e.g., Lipschitz gradients, bounded variance) for the synchronous SPFL algorithm, and discuss the additional challenges in the asynchronous setting.

- Leap gradient approximation derivation is unclear and potentially incorrect: Equations (10) and (11) for the leap gradient approximation are presented without derivation or justification. The paper claims they are inspired by DC-ASGD, but the connection is not explained. The term $\tilde{S}(k)(w_k^{t_0}-w^{t_0})\odot(w_k^{t_0}-w^{t_0})\odot(w^{t_\tau}-w^{t_1})$ is described as an approximation of the gradient of the gradient, but this is not a standard Taylor expansion. The dimensions: $(w_k^{t_0}-w^{t_0})$ is a vector, its element-wise square is a vector, and $(w^{t_\tau}-w^{t_1})$ is also a vector. The element-wise product of three vectors is a vector, which is added to $w^{t_0}$ to estimate $w_k^{t_\tau}$. This does not correspond to any known first-order or second-order approximation. The paper should provide a clear derivation, state the assumptions, and show why this approximation is valid. Without this, the PLGA algorithm is essentially ad hoc.

- Experimental setup lacks realistic heterogeneity and scalability analysis: The experiments use only 10 clients with fixed class partitions (6, 60, 6, 32 classes per client for the four datasets). This is a very small-scale setting. The paper does not vary the number of clients, the degree of non-IIDness (e.g., using Dirichlet distributions with different concentration parameters), or the fraction of stragglers. The asynchronous experiments fix the set of stragglers and their missing periods, which is not realistic—in practice, stragglers appear randomly. The paper should include experiments with more clients (e.g., 50 or 100), different heterogeneity levels, and random straggler patterns. Additionally, the communication and computation overhead of computing the similarity matrix is not reported. The paper claims the algorithms are efficient but provides no measurements of wall-clock time or communication rounds.

- Ablation studies are insufficient to isolate contributions of key components: The paper includes some ablation: comparing SPFL-w (weight-based aggregation) vs SPFL (gradient-based), and 1-step vs 2-step local updates. However, there is no ablation on the core component—the gradient similarity metric itself. For example, how does performance change if one uses a fixed similarity matrix (computed once at initialization) vs updating it every $\Gamma$ rounds? What if one uses cosine similarity without softmax normalization? What if one uses a simpler similarity measure like label distribution overlap? Without these ablations, it is unclear whether the performance gains come from the gradient similarity or from other aspects of the algorithm (e.g., the personalized aggregation in (7) or the stage strategy). The paper should include experiments that replace the gradient similarity with alternative measures.

- Presentation of algorithms and equations is confusing and contains errors: The paper has several presentation issues that make it difficult to evaluate. Algorithm 1 calls `ClientUpdate` twice in the same round, which is unusual and not explained. Equation (12) appears twice with different notation (once with $w_{kp}^{t_\tau}$ and once with $w_k^{t_*}$). The stage strategy is introduced but the subscript $s$ is used inconsistently—in (7) it appears on $w_{si}^{t+1}$ and $g_j^t$, but $g_j^t$ is not defined with a stage subscript. In Algorithm 2, the server aggregates all clients' models as $w^{t+1} = \frac{1}{N'}\sum_{i=0}^{N'} w_i^t$, but this does not use the leap gradient approximation for stragglers—the approximation is computed but then ignored in the aggregation. These inconsistencies suggest the algorithms may not be correctly implemented. The paper should carefully revise all equations and pseudocode to ensure consistency.

- No demonstration that gradient similarity captures data distribution similarity: The paper's central claim is that the Softmax Normalized Gradient Similarity (SNGS) captures the relationship between clients' data distributions. However, there is no experiment or example that validates this claim. The paper should include a controlled synthetic experiment where the true data distribution similarity is known (e.g., clients with known label shifts, such as Dirichlet(0.1) vs Dirichlet(10.0)), and then compute SNGS to show that it correlates with the true similarity. Without this, the reader cannot assess whether the metric is meaningful or just noise. A simple simulation with two clients sharing the same distribution and two clients with disjoint label sets would suffice to demonstrate that SNGS is high for similar distributions and low for dissimilar ones.

- Missing comparison with simpler personalized FL baselines: The paper compares SPFL only against FedAvg, FedUpdate, Per-FedAvg, and pFedMe. It does not compare against simpler personalized FL methods such as local fine-tuning of the global model (i.e., each client trains the global model on its own data for a few extra steps), or against clustering-based methods like IFCA or FedGroup. These baselines are standard in the personalized FL literature and would help isolate whether the complexity of SNGS is necessary. The paper should add at least one such baseline (e.g., 'Global model + local fine-tuning') to its main tables, and discuss how the performance gain justifies the additional computation and communication of SNGS.

- Ablation on the stage strategy is missing: The paper introduces a 'stage strategy' inspired by ResNet, where the similarity matrix is computed per stage of the network. However, there is no ablation study that compares using a single similarity matrix for the whole network versus per-stage matrices. The paper also does not explain how stages are defined for non-ResNet architectures (e.g., for the simple CNNs used on MNIST/EMNIST). The authors should include an experiment that removes the stage strategy (i.e., uses one similarity matrix for the entire model) and reports the accuracy. This would clarify whether the stage strategy is a necessary component or an unnecessary complication.

- Asynchronous FL simulation lacks realistic straggler patterns: The asynchronous experiments fix the set of stragglers and assign them periodic missing patterns (e.g., miss 2 rounds, participate 1 round). This is a highly artificial setup. In practice, stragglers appear randomly due to varying network latency, computation time, or client availability. The paper should include experiments with random straggler patterns (e.g., each client has a probability p of being a straggler in each round, independent across rounds). Additionally, the paper should vary the fraction of stragglers (e.g., 20%, 50%, 80%) to show that PLGA remains effective under different levels of system heterogeneity. Without this, the asynchronous results are not convincing for real-world deployment.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Provide a clear derivation and justification for the leap gradient approximation in equations (10)-(11), or remove it if it cannot be justified.

- Add theoretical convergence analysis for at least the synchronous SPFL algorithm under standard assumptions.

- Expand the experimental evaluation to include more clients (e.g., 50-100), varying degrees of non-IIDness (Dirichlet partitions), and random straggler patterns. Report communication and computation overhead.

- Include ablation studies that isolate the contribution of the gradient similarity metric by comparing with alternative similarity measures (e.g., label distribution overlap, fixed similarity).

- Fix all inconsistencies in the algorithms and equations: clarify the double ClientUpdate call in Algorithm 1, ensure the aggregation in Algorithm 2 uses the leap gradient approximation, and make the stage subscript notation consistent.

comments.title: Leap gradient approximation computed but not used in aggregation
comments.quote:
for the straggers $w_k^t$, do (8), (13), (11), (14) in sequence;

the server aggregates all clients' model: $w^{t+1} = \frac{1}{N'} \sum_{i=0}^{N'} w_i^t$
comments.explanation: The pseudocode computes an approximation of the straggler's model via equations (8), (13), (11), (14), but then aggregates the original $w_i^t$ values without using the approximated ones. This makes the leap gradient computation pointless. Either use the approximated straggler models in the aggregation (e.g., replace $w_k^t$ with the approximated value before averaging) or remove the leap gradient computation from the algorithm.

comments.title: Softmax denominator in equation (13) is not a proper normalization
comments.explanation: Equation (13) defines $\tilde{S}_s(k) = e^{S_s(k)} / (e + e^{S_s(k)})$. This is not a standard softmax normalization: a standard softmax over a set of values would have denominator $\sum_j e^{S_s(j)}$, summing over all clients $j$. The given denominator uses the constant $e$ (Euler's number) plus $e^{S_s(k)}$, which does not normalize the values to sum to 1 across clients. This appears to be a typo; the intended denominator is likely $\sum_j e^{S_s(j)}$ or $1 + e^{S_s(k)}$ if a sigmoid-like form is intended. The current form is mathematically inconsistent with the claim of normalization and will produce incorrect similarity weights. Rewrite equation (13) as $\tilde{S}_s(k) = e^{S_s(k)} / \sum_j e^{S_s(j)}$ (or clarify if a sigmoid is intended and adjust the notation accordingly).

comments.title: ClientUpdate called twice without justification
comments.quote:
if  $t\% \Gamma == 0$  then
$w_{gi}^t = \frac{1}{N}\sum_i^N w_i^t$ $\{g_i^t\} = \text{ClientUpdate}(w_{gi}^t, C_i, \beta)$ $\hat{S}_s = \text{Update}\hat{S}\text{Matrix}(\{g_i^t\})$ $\{g_i^t\} = \text{ClientUpdate}(w_{gi}^t, C_i, \beta)$ $\{w_{si}^{t+1}\} = \text{ServerUpdate}(\{w_{gi}^t\}, \{g_i^t\}, \hat{S}_s, \alpha)$
comments.explanation: The pseudocode calls `ClientUpdate` twice in the same round when `t % Gamma == 0`. The first call computes gradients used to update the SNGS matrix; the second call overwrites `{g_i^t}` and passes the new gradients to `ServerUpdate`. This doubles local computation per round without explanation. The paper should either remove the second call and reuse the first set of gradients, or explicitly justify why two separate local training steps are needed. Rewrite the pseudocode to either use a single `ClientUpdate` call or add a comment explaining the purpose of the second call.

comments.title: ServerUpdate returns undefined variable
comments.quote:
Def ServerUpdate  $(\{w_{si}^t\}, \{g_i^t\}, \hat{S}_s, \alpha)$ :
for  $s \in S$  do
perform (7) to get  $w_{si}^{t+1}$
return  $\{w_{gi}^{t+1}\}$
comments.explanation: The `ServerUpdate` function receives `{w_{si}^t}` and computes `w_{si}^{t+1}` via equation (7), but returns `{w_{gi}^{t+1}}`. The variable `w_{gi}^{t+1}` is never defined in the function body or in the main loop (only `w_{gi}^t` is defined as the average of local models). This is likely a typo: the return should be `{w_{si}^{t+1}}`. If the global model is intended, its computation must be shown. Rewrite the return statement as `return {w_{si}^{t+1}}` or add the missing step that computes `w_{gi}^{t+1}`.

comments.title: Global model w_{gi}^t computed only every Gamma rounds
comments.quote:
if  $t\% \Gamma == 0$  then
$w_{gi}^t = \frac{1}{N}\sum_i^N w_i^t$
comments.explanation: The global model `w_{gi}^t` is computed only when `t % Gamma == 0`. In rounds where `t % Gamma != 0`, the variable `w_{gi}^t` is not defined, yet it is used as input to `ClientUpdate` (which expects `w_{gi}^t`). This means the algorithm is underspecified for non-synchronization rounds. The paper should either define `w_{gi}^t` for all rounds (e.g., by using the previous round's value) or restructure the pseudocode to avoid referencing an undefined variable. Add a default assignment for `w_{gi}^t` in the else branch, or move the global model computation outside the conditional.

comments.title: Gradient subscript inconsistency in equation (7)
comments.explanation: Equation (7) uses $g_j^t$ without a stage subscript, while $w_{si}^{t}$ and $\tilde{S}_s(i,j)$ carry the stage subscript $s$. The following sentence defines $g_{si}^{t}$ as 'the updated gradient ... of the stage $s$ on the client $i$', but the equation uses $g_j^t$. This inconsistency makes it ambiguous whether the gradient is stage-specific or global. If the gradient is stage-specific, the equation should use $g_{sj}^t$ for consistency. If the gradient is the same across stages, the paper should clarify that $g_j^t$ is the full-model gradient. Rewrite (7) as $w_{si}^{t+1} = w_{si}^{t} - \alpha \sum_{j}^{N} \frac{|D_j|}{|D|} \tilde{S}_s(i, j) g_{sj}^t$ to maintain consistency with the stage notation.

comments.title: Duplicate equation numbers (12) and (12)
comments.quote:
$w_{kp}^{t_{\tau}}=w^{t_{0}}+\left(1-\tilde{S}(k)\right)\left(w_{k}^{t_{\tau}}-w^{t_{0}}\right)+\tilde{S}(k)\left(w^{t_{\tau}}-w^{t_{0}}\right).$ (12)

<!-- PAGE BREAK -->

$w_{k}^{t_{*}}=w_{k}^{t_{0}}+\left(1-\tilde{S}_{s}(k)\right)\left(w_{sk}^{t_{*}}-w_{s}^{t_{0}}\right)+\tilde{S}_{s}(k)\left(w_{s}^{t_{*}}-w_{s}^{t_{0}}\right),$ (12)
comments.explanation: The section contains two distinct equations both labeled (12). The first defines $w_{kp}^{t_\tau}$ using $\tilde{S}(k)$ and global iteration superscripts; the second defines $w_k^{t_*}$ using $\tilde{S}_s(k)$ and stage subscripts. These are different mathematical objects with different notation and arguments. This numbering error will confuse readers and hinder cross-referencing. Rewrite the second equation with a new number (e.g., (15)) and update all references accordingly.

comments.title: Undefined symbol $w_{sk}^{t_*}$ in (14)
comments.explanation: Equation (14) uses $w_{sk}^{t_*}$ on the right-hand side, but this symbol is not defined anywhere in the paper. The earlier duplicate equation (12) also uses $w_{sk}^{t_*}$ without definition. The paper defines $w_k^{t_*}$ as the local model after training, but the subscript $s$ (stage) is not attached to it in any prior definition. Without a definition of $w_{sk}^{t_*}$, the equation is incomplete and the algorithm cannot be implemented. Add a definition of $w_{sk}^{t_*}$ (likely the local model of client $k$ after training on stage $s$) before or after equation (14).

comments.title: Hessian approximation is unsubstantiated
comments.quote: especially, $Diag\left(\left(w_{k}^{t_{0}}-w^{t_{0}}\right)\odot\left(w_{k}^{t_{0}}-w^{t_{0}}\right)\right)$ is the approximation of the gradient of the gradient of $w_{k}^{t_{0}}$ .
comments.explanation: The gradient of the gradient (the Hessian) is a $d \times d$ matrix, not a diagonal matrix. The element-wise square of a vector, when placed on the diagonal, yields a diagonal matrix. This is not a standard approximation of the Hessian unless the Hessian is assumed to be diagonal, which is a strong and unstated assumption. The paper should either justify this diagonal approximation (e.g., by assuming a separable loss or using a Gauss-Newton-type approximation) or provide a derivation showing why this term arises from a Taylor expansion. Without such justification, the claim is mathematically unsupported. Rewrite the quoted text to either remove the claim that this is a Hessian approximation or add a justification for the diagonal assumption.

comments.title: Taylor expansion derivation is missing
comments.quote: assuming that the training loss function is $L_{1}$-$Lipschitz$, inspired by DC-ASGD *(Zheng et al., 2017)*, to alleviate the update gap between stragglers and the server, we use the approximation of the first-order Taylor expansion of the gradient to estimate the model of straggler $C_{k}$ in the current server iteration round $\tau$ by (10):
comments.explanation: The paper claims that (10) is derived from a first-order Taylor expansion of the gradient, but no derivation is provided. In DC-ASGD, the Taylor expansion is applied to the gradient of the loss function to approximate delayed gradients. Here, the goal is to approximate the straggler's model $w_k^{t_\tau}$ from $w^{t_0}$ and $w^{t_\tau}$. The expression in (10) does not resemble a standard Taylor expansion: it involves an element-wise product of three vectors scaled by a scalar similarity, plus an additive term. The Lipschitz assumption alone does not justify this form. The paper should either provide a step-by-step derivation showing how (10) follows from a Taylor expansion under stated assumptions, or clarify that this is a heuristic inspired by DC-ASGD rather than a rigorous approximation. Add a derivation or a clear statement that the form is heuristic.

comments.title: Equation (10) uses $w^{t_0}$ but $w^{t_1}$ appears in denominator
comments.explanation: The gradient similarity $S(k)$ in (8) uses $w^{t_1}$, the global model after the first aggregation. However, in (10) the term $(w^{t_\tau} - w^{t_1})$ appears, which involves $w^{t_1}$ as a reference point. If $\tau = 1$, then $w^{t_\tau} - w^{t_1} = 0$, making the entire correction term zero, and (10) reduces to $w_k^{t_1} = w^{t_0} + (w_k^{t_0} - w^{t_0}) = w_k^{t_0}$. This means that at the first aggregation round, the predicted straggler model is just its initial local model, which is inconsistent with the idea that the straggler has been training locally. The paper should discuss this edge case or adjust the formula to handle $\tau=1$ properly.

comments.title: Inconsistent variable names in ClientUpdate
comments.quote:
**Def ClientUpdate** $(w^t, C_k, \beta)$:

$w_i^{t-1} = w^t$

for $e = 0; e < E; e++$ do

for $b \in B$ do
$w_k^t \gets w_k^{t-1} - \beta \nabla L_k(f(w_k^{t-1}, x_b), y_b)$

return $w_k^t$
comments.explanation: The ClientUpdate function initializes $w_i^{t-1} = w^t$ but then updates $w_k^t$ in the loop, using $w_k^{t-1}$ which is never initialized. The variable $i$ appears only in the initialization and is never used again. The function returns $w_k^t$, but $k$ is not a local variable — it is presumably the client index passed as $C_k$, but the notation is inconsistent. The loop variable $b$ iterates over $B$ (the batch size), but $B$ is a scalar, not a set — the pseudocode should iterate over mini-batches drawn from the local dataset. This makes the local update step ambiguous and potentially incorrect. The paper should rewrite ClientUpdate with consistent variable names (e.g., $w \gets w - \beta \nabla L_k(\ldots)$) and clarify how mini-batches are sampled.

comments.title: Incorrect claim about Per-FedAvg best on IID except EMNIST
comments.quote: SPFL performs much better on four non-IID datasets, compared to FedAvg, FedUpdate and Per-FedAvg. For IID datasets, Per-FedAvg has the best results except on EMNIST dataset. Nonetheless, the performance gap between SPFL and the best methods is still acceptable on IID datasets.
comments.explanation: The text states that Per-FedAvg has the best results on IID datasets except on EMNIST. However, Table 1 shows that on IID EMNIST, Per-FedAvg achieves 91.27% while SPFL achieves 89.53%, so Per-FedAvg is still best. The claim is contradicted by the table. Correct the text to say that Per-FedAvg outperforms SPFL on all four IID datasets.

comments.title: Twice-update claim contradicted by EMNIST non-IID result
comments.quote: The experimental results also show that the twice local updates dominate its counterpart, which indicate that MAML alike updates is helpful in personalization than once local update.
comments.explanation: The text claims that twice local updates (SPFL) dominate once local updates (SPFL (1-step)). However, Table 1 shows that on non-IID EMNIST, SPFL (1-step) achieves 87.66% while SPFL achieves 87.49%, so the 1-step version is slightly better. This contradicts the claim. The paper should either explain why EMNIST is an exception or revise the claim to acknowledge that the benefit of twice updates is not universal across all datasets.

comments.title: Inconsistent abbreviation: PLPA vs PLGA
comments.quote: In the asynchronous FL, the proposed PLPA is compared with the FedAvg (in the synchronous and asynchronous conditions), FedAsync and Per-FedAvg.
comments.explanation: The text introduces the algorithm as 'PLPA' here, but the section title, the rest of the paragraph, and the table all refer to 'PLGA'. This is an inconsistency that will confuse readers. The abbreviation should be unified to 'PLGA' throughout. Rewrite 'PLPA' as 'PLGA' in the quoted sentence.

comments.title: Unclear reference to LPA and PFGA
comments.quote: We also notice that PLGA is better than LPA as a result of personalization, and that  $w_{k}^{t_{s}}$  computed by (11) is better than by (10) for PFGA.
comments.explanation: The abbreviations 'LPA' and 'PFGA' are not defined in this section or earlier in the paper (the paper defines 'LGA' and 'PLGA'). This makes the sentence incomprehensible. The authors should define these terms or correct them to the intended abbreviations (likely 'LGA' and 'SPFL' or similar). Replace 'LPA' with 'LGA' and 'PFGA' with the correct abbreviation (e.g., 'SPFL' if that is the intended method), or define them explicitly.

comments.title: Missing variance reporting for results
comments.quote: We use 10 seeds to get a average result for each method.
comments.explanation: The paper states that 10 seeds are used to obtain an average result, but it does not report any measure of variance (e.g., standard deviation, standard error, or confidence intervals) for the accuracy results. In federated learning experiments, results can vary significantly across seeds due to random data splits, client sampling, and initialization. Without variance information, readers cannot assess the statistical significance of the reported improvements. The authors should include standard deviations or confidence intervals for all reported accuracy numbers, either in the main tables or in an appendix.

comments.title: Claim about SPFL-w outperforming independent training is unsupported
comments.quote: Compared to FedAvg and independent training of each client in Appendix B, SPFL outperforms both no matter on IID datasets or non-IID datasets. To differentiate the influence of gradient and weight aggregation strategy, we observed that SPFL-w is slightly better in the case of IID data, but SPFL is much better than SPFL-w on non-IID data. Nonetheless, SPFL-w outperforms the FedAvg and the independent training model both on IID and non-IID datasets.
comments.explanation: The text claims that SPFL-w outperforms independent training, but the independent training results are only in Appendix B, not provided in the main text or Table 1. Without those results, the claim cannot be verified from the provided section. The paper should either include the independent training results in the main table or remove the unsupported claim. Rewrite the sentence to remove the reference to independent training or add the results to the main text.

comments.title: Stage subscript s usage is underspecified
comments.quote:
for  $s \in S$  do
perform (7) to get  $w_{si}^{t+1}$
return  $\{w_{gi}^{t+1}\}$
comments.explanation: The `ServerUpdate` function iterates over stages `s`, but the inputs `{w_{si}^t}` and `{g_i^t}` are not indexed by stage. The SNGS matrix `\hat{S}_s` is stage-specific, but it is unclear whether the same gradients `{g_i^t}` are used for all stages or separate gradients are computed per stage. The algorithm should clarify how the stage index `s` is used. Add a description or modify the pseudocode to show that `{g_i^t}` and `{w_{si}^t}` are stage-dependent, or explain that the same gradients are reused across stages.

comments.title: Additive term in (11) is not updated over time
comments.quote: The only difference between (10) and (11) is their first terms on their RHS. In (11), it says that we want to predict $w_{k}^{t_{\tau}}$ based on current global model $w^{t_{\tau}}$, yet still use the prediction of the changes of $w_{k}^{t_{0}}$ in the previous FL iteration round.
comments.explanation: The additive term $(w_k^{t_0} - w^{t_0})$ in both (10) and (11) is a fixed vector from the initial round. If the goal is to predict the straggler's model at round $\tau$, one would expect the prediction to depend on the current global model and the straggler's past update, but this additive term is not updated over time. This suggests that the approximation does not account for the straggler's local training after round 0, which is a significant limitation. The paper should clarify whether $w_k^{t_0}$ is the only local model available from the straggler, and if so, why this approximation is reasonable. Add a discussion of this limitation or modify the formula to incorporate updates.

comments.title: Missing definition of $\tilde{S}$ and $w_k^{t_*}$ in (12)
comments.quote: where $\tilde{S}$ and $w_{k}^{t_{*}}$ are derived from (9) and (10) or (11), respectively.
comments.explanation: The text states that $\tilde{S}$ and $w_k^{t_*}$ are derived from (9) and (10) or (11), but the paper does not include equations (9), (10), or (11) in the provided excerpt. Without these definitions, the first equation (12) is incomplete and the reader cannot understand how $\tilde{S}(k)$ or $w_k^{t_*}$ are computed. This is a scope gap: the proof relies on definitions not present in the section. Add the missing equations (9)–(11) or provide a clear reference to where they appear earlier in the paper.

comments.title: Unclear relationship between $\tilde{S}$ and $\tilde{S}_s$
comments.quote:
Similar to (6), we can also apply stage strategy to $\tilde{S}(i)$, and obtain:

$\tilde{S}_{s}(k)=\frac{e^{S_{s}(k)}}{e+e^{S_{s}(k)}},$ (13)
comments.explanation: The text says the stage strategy is applied to $\tilde{S}(i)$ to obtain $\tilde{S}_s(k)$, but the relationship between $\tilde{S}(i)$ and $S_s(k)$ is not defined. The notation $S_s(k)$ appears without explanation; it is presumably a stage-wise similarity score, but its definition is missing. Without this, the derivation from $\tilde{S}(i)$ to $\tilde{S}_s(k)$ is unjustified. Add a definition of $S_s(k)$ and explain how it relates to the original similarity measure $S(k)$ used in (9).

comments.title: Table 2 server accuracy for PLGA on CIFAR10 is lower than client accuracy
comments.explanation: In Table 2, for PLGA on CIFAR10, the client accuracy (46.11%) is higher than the server accuracy (45.96%). This is unusual because the server model is typically a global model that should have lower accuracy on the non-IID client data than the personalized client model. However, the paper does not explain this inversion. Moreover, for all other methods on CIFAR10, the server accuracy is higher than the client accuracy (e.g., FedAvg (Async): client 42.09%, server 46.52%). The authors should explain why PLGA's server accuracy is lower than its client accuracy, or verify that the numbers are correct. Add a brief explanation for this inversion or correct the numbers if they are erroneous.

comments.title: Missing justification for straggler simulation period values
comments.quote: For stragglers in  $S$ , we let them missing server aggregation round periodically with different fixed missing period as  $1, 2, \ldots, |S|$ , where the values correspond to the number of server iteration rounds.
comments.explanation: The text assigns missing periods 1, 2, ..., |S| to the stragglers, but does not explain which straggler gets which period or why this assignment is representative. Without a clear mapping, the simulation may not be reproducible. Specify the assignment rule (e.g., straggler i gets period i) and justify that this covers realistic asynchronous behavior.

comments.title: Scope gap: only |S|=5 tested, no variation reported
comments.quote: The experimental results in Table 2, where the total number of the stragglers is  $|S| = 5$ , show that the clients' performances of PLGA on the four non-IID datasets have a notable advantage over the other baseline algorithms.
comments.explanation: The paper only evaluates with |S|=5 stragglers out of N=10 clients. The abstract claims PLGA handles heterogeneous asynchronous FL, but no results are shown for other straggler counts (e.g., |S|=0, 2, 8). The claim of 'notable advantage' may not generalize. Add experiments with varying |S| to demonstrate robustness, or explicitly limit the claim to the tested configuration.

comments.title: Gradient sign convention needs clarification
comments.quote:
Then the SGD updates of the corresponding gradients of $w^{t}_{i}$ and $w^{t}_{j}$ are:

\[ \begin{split}&g^{t}_{i}=w^{t}-w^{t}_{i},\\
&g^{t}_{j}=w^{t}-w^{t}_{j}.\end{split} \] (2)
comments.explanation:
The definition $g_i^t = w^t - w_i^t$ is the negative of the actual gradient scaled by the local learning rate. In standard SGD, $w_i^t = w^t - \eta 
abla L_i(w^t)$, so $
abla L_i(w^t) = (w^t - w_i^t)/\eta$. The paper uses $g_i^t$ as the negative gradient scaled by $\eta$. This sign convention is used consistently in (3) and (4), but it should be explicitly stated to avoid confusion. Rewrite (2) as $g_i^t = w^t - w_i^t$, where $g_i^t$ approximates $\eta 
abla L_i(w^t)$ (the negative gradient scaled by the local learning rate).

comments.title: Softmax normalization yields asymmetric matrix
comments.explanation: The softmax normalization in (5) sums over the second index $j$ only, producing a row-stochastic matrix where each row sums to 1. Since the cosine similarity $S(i,j)$ in (3) is symmetric, the resulting matrix $\hat{S}$ is not symmetric: $\hat{S}(i,j) \neq \hat{S}(j,i)$ in general. The paper does not discuss whether this asymmetry is intentional or how it affects the aggregation in (4) and (7). If the goal is to produce a row-stochastic matrix, this is fine, but the paper should explicitly state that $\hat{S}$ is row-normalized and not symmetric. Additionally, the notation $\hat{S}$ in (5) is inconsistent with the later use of $\tilde{S}$ in (6) and (7); the paper should use a consistent symbol (e.g., $\tilde{S}$ throughout). Rewrite (5) as $\tilde{S}(i,j) = \frac{e^{S(i,j)}}{\sum_{k=1}^N e^{S(i,k)}}$ and clarify that this yields a row-stochastic matrix.

comments.title: Stage-specific notation introduced without definition
comments.explanation: The term $w_{si}^{t}$ appears in (7) but has not been defined previously. Earlier in the section, the paper uses $w_i^t$ to denote the local model of client $i$ after local update at round $t$. The stage-specific notation $w_{si}^{t}$ is introduced here without explanation of how it relates to $w_i^t$ or how stages partition the model parameters. The paper should define $w_{si}^{t}$ explicitly, e.g., as the parameters of stage $s$ of client $i$'s model at round $t$, and explain how stages are defined for non-ResNet architectures. Add a definition before (7): 'Let $w_{si}^{t}$ denote the parameters of stage $s$ of client $i$'s model at round $t$, and let $g_{si}^{t}$ be the corresponding gradient update.'

comments.title: Similarity matrix update procedure is underspecified
comments.quote: Therefore, the similarity matrix is updated regularly during the training process. During each update, the model parameters of all clients are aggregated with equal weight to get $w_t$ of (2), and the similarity matrix is calculated based on $w_t$.
comments.explanation: The paper states that the similarity matrix is updated 'regularly' but does not specify the frequency (e.g., every $T$ rounds, or after each round). Additionally, the procedure for computing $w_t$ by aggregating all client models with equal weight is described, but it is unclear whether this aggregation uses the local models $w_i^t$ after local updates or some other quantity. The phrase 'model parameters of all clients are aggregated with equal weight to get $w_t$ of (2)' is ambiguous: does it mean $w_t = \frac{1}{N} \sum_i w_i^t$? If so, this should be stated explicitly. Without a precise specification, the algorithm is not reproducible. Add a clear description: 'Every $K$ rounds, the server computes $w_t = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^N w_i^t$ and recalculates $S(i,j)$ using (3) with this $w_t$.'

comments.title: SPFL-w (1-s) row missing from table description
comments.explanation: The table includes a row labeled 'SPFL-w (1-s)' but the text only mentions 'SPFL-w (1-step)' and 'SPFL (1-step)'. The abbreviation '(1-s)' is not defined in the text, which may confuse readers. Add a definition or expand the abbreviation in the table caption or the main text.

coarse__glm-4.7-flash.label: Coarse (glm-4.7-flash)
coarse__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes two algorithms, SPFL and PLGA, for personalized federated learning to handle statistical and system heterogeneity. The empirical results show competitive performance, particularly on non-IID data. However, the theoretical underpinnings of the PLGA algorithm are mathematically suspect, the novelty claims regarding asynchronous personalization are overstated, and the presentation of the stage strategy contribution is incomplete.

The work demonstrates empirical improvements over standard baselines, which is a positive sign for practical applicability. However, the manuscript suffers from significant theoretical gaps and ambiguous descriptions of the proposed methods. The mathematical derivation of the gradient approximation in the asynchronous setting appears incorrect, and the novelty of the approach is not clearly established against existing literature. The paper would benefit from a rigorous theoretical analysis and a clearer articulation of the communication protocol.

Overview issues:

- Mathematical Inconsistency in PLGA Gradient Approximation: The derivation of the Leap Gradient Approximation (LGA) in Section 3.2 relies on Equation (10), which claims to utilize a first-order Taylor expansion of the gradient. However, the equation involves squaring the parameter deltas, $(w_{k}^{t_{0}} - w^{t_{0}}) 	ext{ and } (w_{k}^{t_{0}} - w^{t_{0}})$, which is mathematically inconsistent with a standard Taylor expansion. A first-order Taylor expansion of a function $f(x)$ involves the derivative at a point, not the square of the input difference. This suggests the theoretical justification for the PLGA method is fundamentally flawed, even if the empirical results happen to be positive. The authors must re-derive this approximation correctly or provide a valid mathematical basis for the proposed update rule.

- Ambiguous Personalized Model Transmission in SPFL: The abstract and introduction state that the server 'sends the personalized global model to each client' (Section 1, Abstract). However, the update rule in Equation (4) and the algorithm description (Alg. 1) suggest that the personalization occurs locally on the client side through the update rule $w^{t+1}_{i}=w^{t}_{i}-	ext{weighted sum of gradients}$. It is unclear whether the server explicitly transmits a new personalized model to the client in each round, or if the client simply updates its local model based on the server's returned gradients. This ambiguity obscures the contribution of the method and makes it difficult to analyze the communication overhead.

- Overstated Novelty Regarding Personalization in Asynchronous FL: The abstract claims that the work is 'one of the few studies investigating explicitly on personalization in asynchronous FL.' This claim is likely incorrect. Recent literature, such as Ditto (Li et al., 2021), explicitly addresses personalization in federated learning frameworks. Furthermore, the related work section acknowledges Ditto and FedAsync, which are competitive baselines. Citing these works and clarifying how the proposed method differs from existing async personalization approaches is necessary to establish novelty and avoid overstating the contribution.

- Missing Main-Text Evidence for Stage Strategy Contribution: The paper introduces the 'stage strategy of ResNet' as a contribution to improve the performance of both SPFL and PLGA (Abstract, Section 3). However, the experimental results in Section 4.1 and 4.2 do not explicitly present tables or figures comparing the performance of the stage strategy against the base SPFL or PLGA. The text mentions an ablation study in Appendix C, but the primary contribution should be demonstrated in the main text to ensure reviewers can verify its impact without digging into supplementary material.

- Unrealistic Straggler Modeling in PLGA Experiments: The PLGA algorithm aims to address 'systems heterogeneity' by handling stragglers. However, the experimental setup in Section 4.2 simulates stragglers using a periodic missing strategy (missing 1, 2, ..., |S| rounds). This is a highly deterministic and artificial pattern that does not reflect the stochastic nature of stragglers in real-world networks. A robust evaluation should include random straggler patterns or varying straggler probabilities to validate the algorithm's effectiveness against genuine system heterogeneity.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Correct the mathematical derivation of Equation (10) in Section 3.2 to ensure it is a valid Taylor expansion or provide a rigorous proof for the proposed update rule.

- Clarify the communication protocol in SPFL: explicitly state whether the server sends a distinct personalized model to the client in each round or if the update is purely local.

- Re-evaluate the novelty claim regarding personalization in asynchronous FL by providing a more detailed comparison with recent works like Ditto and FedAsync.

- Add a main-text table or figure demonstrating the impact of the ResNet stage strategy contribution, rather than relegating it to the appendix.

- Expand the PLGA experiments to include random straggler patterns to better simulate real-world system heterogeneity.

comments.title: Contradictory Claim About TWAFL Handling Staleness
comments.quote: In TWAFL *(Chen et al., 2019)*, a time-weighted aggregation strategy is used by server to handle stragglers according to the staleness of the received model parameters. Although these asynchronous FL algorithms can overcome some difficulties encountered in synchronous aggregation, such as waiting for the response from client, they are still inefficient for directly aggregating the received local model on the server, without taking the iteration gap of stragglers’ local models at global communication round into consideration.
comments.explanation: The text claims TWAFL handles staleness via time-weighted aggregation, but then immediately argues these algorithms are inefficient because they do not account for the iteration gap. This is a contradiction; TWAFL is explicitly designed to handle staleness. The authors should clarify why existing methods are insufficient compared to their approach, rather than falsely claiming they ignore staleness.

comments.title: Contradictory Statement on Variable Derivation
comments.quote: where $\tilde{S}$ and $w_{k}^{t_{*}}$ are derived from (9) and (10) or (11), respectively.
comments.explanation: The text states that $w_{k}^{t_{*}}$ is derived from (10) or (11), yet the equation immediately following defines $w_{k}^{t_{*}}$ explicitly as a convex combination of $w_{sk}^{t_{*}}$ and $w_{s}^{t_{0}}$. This creates a contradiction or confusion about the source of the variable. The authors should clarify whether $w_{k}^{t_{*}}$ is defined by this equation or if the equation is meant to define a different variable.

comments.title: Logical Inconsistency in SPFL Description
comments.quote: Different from the aforementioned methods, we propose a Subclass Personalized Federated Learning (SPFL) algorithm, which utilizes the Softmax Normalized Gradient Similarity (SNGS) to generate global models with each specific to one client for its parameter fine-tune.
comments.explanation: The phrasing 'generate global models with each specific to one client' is confusing. A global model is shared, while a model specific to one client is personalized. This phrasing suggests the server sends distinct models to each client, which contradicts the standard FL paradigm and likely the actual implementation (local fine-tuning on a shared model). Rewrite to clarify that the method generates a shared global model which is then fine-tuned locally for each client, or that personalized models are generated based on the global model.
